title: Error checking for blob at destination when pushing some containers to an OSTree repo
body:
/kind bug

**DISCLAIMER:** I will admit I'm an idiot and am trying this with podman 0.10.1, *but* in my defense, there don't seem to have been any commits related to or potentially fixing this in the master branch.

When pushing some containers to an OSTree repo, I'll get errors like this:

```
Error copying image to the remote destination: Error committing the finished image: lchown /tmp/redis_3Alatest/fd64e64aa7912aba458094873edf9b1a6126fe5356e35a682fb7391c14345520/root/dev/random: no such file or directory
```

This is while *Storing signatures*.

**Reproducing**

The easiest way I've found to reproduce is with [the Docker Redis container](https://hub.docker.com/_/redis/). Just run:

```
$ podman push redis "ostree:redis@$PWD/repo"
```

where `$PWD/repo` is an initialized OSTree repo (`ostree init --repo=repo`).

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:       0.10.1
Go Version:    go1.11
OS/Arch:       linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info`:**

```

      commit: 5d93e7c8cfb873c278af9476544e159dfd1228c7
      spec: 1.0.0
  SwapFree: 8339845120
  SwapTotal: 8359243776
  arch: amd64
  cpus: 4
  hostname: fedora.refi64.com
  kernel: 4.18.16-300.fc29.x86_64
  os: linux
  uptime: 4h 50m 41.34s (Approximately 0.17 days)
insecure registries:

  ContainerStore:
    number: 95
  GraphDriverName: vfs
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

This is on a physical system running Fedora Silverblue 29.

comments.body: Sounds like this could be a c/image issue - @mtrmac @umohnani8 PTAL

FWIW an unprivileged
```console
$ ./skopeo --debug copy docker://redis ostree:redis@$PWD/ostree-repo
```
does not fail.

Based on the path, the failure happens somewhere in `c/image/ostree/ostreeImageDestination.ImportBlob`, likely in `archive.UntarPath` — but I can’t see how it could happen: 

`docker.io/library/redis` (as of `@sha256:4da5768becdde8b439a4af1a4f45624d4bc635edfbcbe60b25402f0cb71a5e61`) does not contain `/dev/random`; only the `/dev` directory exists in the layer blobs.

@giuseppe :

- The `/tmp/redis_3Alatest/` shows that the OSTree transport defaults to using predictable file names inside `/tmp`; that needs fixing.
- Is https://github.com/containers/image/pull/263 really the right thing to do? I can’t see _how_ it would break things unless there were some file lists that , but it looks suspicious.

@kirbyfan64 

- Can you attach the full output of `podman --log-level=debug push …` ?
- What version of the `ostree-libs` RPM do you have installed?
- How _exactly_, starting from fresh storage, did you get the `redis` image? Is this just a `podman pull redis; podman push redis ostree:…`? Or are there some locally added layers?

> Can you attach the full output of `podman --log-level=debug push … `?


> What version of the `ostree-libs` RPM do you have installed?

2018.8

> How *exactly*, starting from fresh storage, did you get the `redis` image? Is this just a `podman pull redis; podman push redis ostree:…`? Or are there some locally added layers?

Just a `podman pull redis`. FWIW I haven't tried this with Docker in any way (don't have it installed on Silverblue); it's installed in podman's local OCI storage.

Hum, this works fine for me on a fresh F29 Silverblue, without updates (podman-0.10.1-1.gite4a1553.fc29.x86_64 ostree-2018.8-1.fc29.x86_64) as well as with updates (podman-0.10.1-1.gite4a1553.fc29.x86_64 ostree-2018.9-1.fc29.x86_64).

And I see a different `redis:latest` image: `@sha256:4da5768becdde8b439a4af1a4f45624d4bc635edfbcbe60b25402f0cb71a5e61` currently has config digest `1babb1dde7e1fc7520ce56ce6d39843a074151bb192522b1988c65a067b15e96` , not the `b12b2cdf20e65f6728f18fc028a4c3466861e2ed0040ba5131575c4b830dfeb8` value from your debug log. AFAICS none of the other recent images (4.0.11, 4.0.10, 4.0.9, 5.0-rc[456]) match either.

What does `podman inspect redis` say?

@mtrmac Hmm, my Redis was rather old, and an upgraded version won't reproduce the same issue. However, `bitnami/redis` encounters a nearly identical issue: 

```
Error copying image to the remote destination: Error committing the finished image: lchown /tmp/redis_3Alatest/7a7427200a8e7babbe6d2a706a30633edc0e4720588909ee376ddabf2965227a/root/dev/full: no such file or directory
```

as does `crystallang/crystal`:

```
Error copying image to the remote destination: Error committing the finished image: lchown /tmp/redis_3Alatest/b8c891f0ffec910a12757d733b178e3f62d81dbbde2b31d3b754071c416108ed/root/dev/agpgart: no such file or directory
```

in rootless mode I am not even able to pull `docker.io/bitnami/redis`.  I get this error:

```
$ podman pull docker.io/bitnami/redis
Trying to pull docker.io/bitnami/redis...Getting image source signatures
Copying blob sha256:f7c4b0c6a5b7b7ff28b83bb5ccfcc7dcadd01a36d0792cebdd99f0319e3cde31
 21.03 MB / 21.03 MB [======================================================] 1s
Copying blob sha256:baafd2181eea36c95cf480f97b1586a7abc5aa3e1a3bdc554effed499c11ae7d
 116 B / 116 B [============================================================] 0s
Copying blob sha256:293b1071f24f9175e9b82fb573fae18bc306a31cfc4c7e28ced29bbb45186ce4
 9.91 MB / 9.91 MB [========================================================] 0s
Copying blob sha256:4af429cf146ff7a016da118d5ddf0b3297ed69662428cdf2576ba63333069203
 4.12 MB / 4.12 MB [========================================================] 0s
Copying blob sha256:4afff65df7650df5d00b0ab502daec9e503d1d2aeaed0eee6e6c34fcb32e29a4

Copying blob sha256:f5408f9933ab04243d8e0037dcfc68393660f3668abf498aee5b8c516d88ed40
 2.42 MB / 2.42 MB [========================================================] 0s
Copying blob sha256:d321db56a4cbf8ba39c6586dd979a400ccdb076fad8e7aeaa16fe66cfa8c0620
 3.38 KB / 3.38 KB [========================================================] 0s
Copying blob sha256:17d6644eff7f06091e69306e660bc9309515b37068be101b29f681ee4140f6d8

Copying config sha256:62dae549efa3c662330022aa9c54d3f59f1082051980e98b9165dafd1c286cc2
 5.24 KB / 5.24 KB [========================================================] 0s
Writing manifest to image destination
Storing signatures
Failed
error pulling image "docker.io/bitnami/redis": unable to pull docker.io/bitnami/redis: unable to pull image: Error committing the finished image: error adding layer with blob "sha256:f7c4b0c6a5b7b7ff28b83bb5ccfcc7dcadd01a36d0792cebdd99f0319e3cde31": Error processing tar file(exit status 1): operation not permitted
``` 

The issue happens as the image includes a device in a layer (`/dev/full`) and that cannot be created by a non privileged user, even in an user namespace (it can be created only on a owned file system, e.g. tmpfs).

For the ostree backend, as @mtrmac pointed out, we clean up these files when they are included in a layer as at that time the centos base image was including devices as well and that was breaking too many images so worth a workaround.

How did you pull locally the  `docker.io/bitnami/redis` as an unprivileged user?
Hmm, I just ran `podman pull bitnami/redis` and it worked...FWIW I'm on podman `0.10.1`.

> Hmm, I just ran `podman pull bitnami/redis` and it worked...FWIW I'm on podman `0.10.1`.

looks like the 'vfs' backend doesn't honor the device, differently than overlay.

Actually they are two different issues, I've opened the PRs here:

https://github.com/containers/storage/pull/237

https://github.com/containers/image/pull/532

> The issue happens as the image includes a device in a layer (/dev/full) and that cannot be created by a non privileged user, even in an user namespace (it can be created only on a owned file system, e.g. tmpfs).

Hum
- Is it semantically correct to just discard device files and start the container anyway? Wouldn’t it be more correct to just refuse to run (pull?) images with devices unprivileged, or refuse to run it if it uses different devices than the ones that can actually be set up, or something like that?
- What happens on a push, or when building on top of the image with discarded devices, and pushing the result? Do the devices just disappear? Or is the FS diff code somehow sufficiently loose that the missing devices are not noticed?

I'd say it is fine to just discard devices.  It is similar to mounting the rootfs with `nodev` which is what we do by default.

@rhatdan @nalind what do you think?
I agree I think it would be ok to just warn on this not block.

The headers that we save using tar-split while applying layers should still include entries for device nodes, so when we regenerate the layer blobs using the headers, they'll still be there, even if they weren't on disk.  Any layer diffs we generate will be based on comparing/examining the layers as they are on-disk, so I wouldn't expect any entries denoting the devices being deleted to be present in the layers of images that are based on images that contain devices.
